coredata.dc:title: Nanocatalyst coupled with a latent-ratiometric electrochemical switch for label-free zero-tolerance rapid detection of live Salmonella in whole blood samples
coredata.dc:creator: Kumaragurubaran, Namasivayam
Arul, P.
Huang, Sheng-Tung
Huang, Chih-Hung
Fang, Shiuh-Bin
Lin, Ying-Hsiu
coredata.dc:description: Developing a simple and rapid analytical tool for detecting live Salmonella is urgently needed for clinical diagnosis and food surveillance. In our previous studies, an activity-based latent ratiometric electrochemical switch was successfully deployed to monitor Salmonella but it could not achieve "zero-tolerance" detection; thus, improving the detection sensitivity from that of our previous approach is needed in order to obtain a practical tool for detecting Salmonella. In this study, a latent ratiometric electrochemical switch Sal-CAF coupled with graphene quantum dots-gold nanoparticles (GQDs-AuNPs) modified electrode was developed to meet the needs of selective detection of live Salmonella. Stable and uniformly sized GQDs-AuNPs (14.25 ± 0.2 nm) were achieved by using a free of reducing agent. The Sal-CAF coupled with GQDs-AuNPs/GCE showed an enhanced current response with an extended linear Salmonella detection concentration range of 1.0 × 103 to 1.0 × 1010 CFU/mL and a limit of detection of 35.62 × 101 CFU/mL within 4 h. Moreover, the detection of 100 CFU/mL of the bacteria could be achieved through a dynamic monitoring method in under 12 h without sample pretreatment or enrichment. Additionally, combining GQDs-AuNPs/GCE with the Sal-CAF platform achieved excellent selectivity, serotypes of pathogenic bacteria, detection of dry-emphasized Salmonella without carrying out a pre-enrichment process, the ability to distinguish live from dead cells, and the ability to work in turbid whole blood media. The present approach constitutes a new achievement for the concept of "zero-tolerance," as it can be used to detect bacterial pathogens ahead of infection control.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Salmonella
Electrochemical switch
Zero-tolerance
Dry-emphasized
Bacterial-pathogens

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of the latent organic probe, graphene quantum dots and nanocomposite
Assay method for the detection of live Salmonella
Analytical and morphological elucidation of GQDs, and GQDs-AuNPs
Electrochemical behavior of Sal-CAF coupled with GQDs-AuNPs modified electrode to sense Salmonella
Ultrasensitive detection performance of live Salmonella
Effect of selectivity, serotype Salmonella bacteria and virulent live from non-virulent dead Salmonella analysis
Dynamic monitoring and zero-tolerance detection of live Salmonella
Real-time detection of Salmonella in a simulated solid surface and human whole blood analysis

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Ministry of Science and Technology, Taiwan
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Ministry of Science and Technology, Taiwan

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors are grateful for financial support from the Ministry of Science and Technology, Taiwan ( MOST-107-2113-M-027-006 and MOST-108-2113-M-027-001 ). P. Arul would like to thank the National Taipei University of Technology for the position of Research Assistant Professor.

The authors are grateful for financial support from the Ministry of Science and Technology, Taiwan (MOST-107-2113-M-027-006 and MOST-108-2113-M-027-001). P. Arul would like to thank the National Taipei University of Technology for the position of Research Assistant Professor.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: HR-TEM images of GQDs-AuNPs with higher magnifications (a, b and c). SAED pattern of GQDs-AuNPs (d).
Deconvoluted XPS spectra of GQDs-AuNPs. Au 4f (a), C 1s (b), N 1s (c) and O 1s (d).

(A) CVs and (B) DPVs for 50 µM of Sal-CAF in the absence (curve a), and presence of Salmonella enteritidis (Sal. E, 1.0 × 1010 CFU/mL) at GQDs-AuNPs/GCE for 4 h (curve c) and control GCE for 7 h (curve d) and 50 µM of CAF alone (curve b). (C) DPVs for 50 µM of Sal-CAF with a linear range concentration of Sal. E (1.0 × 103 to 1.0 × 1010 CFU/mL) on GQDs-AuNPs/GCE. All samples were used in DMSO/PBS (5 % v/v) at 37ᵒC. (D) The plot of current vs. concentrations of Sal. E (CFU/mL) (n = 5).

(A) Bar chart profile for current responses of Sal-CAF against various interfering species of pathogenic bacteria. (B) Plot results for current responses of Sal-CAF (50 µM) in Sal. E cells (106 CFU/mL), chemically and thermally treated deactivated Salmonella.

(A) Plot of analytically found response. Current signal of Sal. E versus incubation time for various concentrations (100 to 105 CFU/mL) (n = 5) in DMSO/PBS (5 % v/v). (B) Photographic images of control (a), 100 CFU/mL (b), and 101 CFU/mL of Sal. E (c) subjected to the standard plating method for 72 h. (C) A plot of the threshold current time (0.01 µA) vs. concentration of Sal. E.

(A and B) Scheme showing the working flowchart for dry emphasized Salmonella recovered from glass substrate with corresponding current signal linear plots. (C) The practical results of whole blood 20 % containing 101 and 102 CFU/mL of Sal. E with 50 µM of Sal-CAF against co-incubation time in DMSO/PBS (5 % v/v) at 37ᵒC. Inset: Photographic image of blood analysis.
Scheme showing the working pathway for the detection of live Salmonella.

head.ce:title: Nanocatalyst coupled with a latent-ratiometric electrochemical switch for label-free zero-tolerance rapid detection of live Salmonella in whole blood samples

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Master Program for Clinical Genomics and Proteomics, College of Pharmacy, Taipei Medical University, Taipei, Taiwan, ROC

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Developing a simple and rapid analytical tool for detecting live Salmonella is urgently needed for clinical diagnosis and food surveillance. In our previous studies, an activity-based latent ratiometric electrochemical switch was successfully deployed to monitor Salmonella but it could not achieve "zero-tolerance" detection; thus, improving the detection sensitivity from that of our previous approach is needed in order to obtain a practical tool for detecting Salmonella. In this study, a latent ratiometric electrochemical switch Sal-CAF coupled with graphene quantum dots-gold nanoparticles (GQDs-AuNPs) modified electrode was developed to meet the needs of selective detection of live Salmonella. Stable and uniformly sized GQDs-AuNPs (14.25 ± 0.2 nm) were achieved by using a free of reducing agent. The Sal-CAF coupled with GQDs-AuNPs/GCE showed an enhanced current response with an extended linear Salmonella detection concentration range of 1.0 × 103 to 1.0 × 1010 CFU/mL and a limit of detection of 35.62 × 101 CFU/mL within 4 h. Moreover, the detection of 100 CFU/mL of the bacteria could be achieved through a dynamic monitoring method in under 12 h without sample pretreatment or enrichment. Additionally, combining GQDs-AuNPs/GCE with the Sal-CAF platform achieved excellent selectivity, serotypes of pathogenic bacteria, detection of dry-emphasized Salmonella without carrying out a pre-enrichment process, the ability to distinguish live from dead cells, and the ability to work in turbid whole blood media. The present approach constitutes a new achievement for the concept of "zero-tolerance," as it can be used to detect bacterial pathogens ahead of infection control.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Nanoelectrocatalyst coupled with a latent ratiometric electrochemical switch for the ultrasensitive and selective detection of live Salmonella in whole blood samples.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors do not have permission to share data.

ce:section.ce:para: Economic growth and human health are threatened by foodborne diseases [1,2]. Salmonella (Sal) is the most common foodborne pathogen and has been associated with food products and infectious diseases [1,3]. The growing number of outbreaks of a seemingly uncountable number of diseases has been strongly connected to Salmonella-contaminated foods [3,4]. The severe symptoms and mortality rate of live cells are uncertain with foodborne diseases, but usually results will appear 12–72 h after the consumption of bacterial-contaminated food or drinks [3]. According to a report from the World Health Organization (WHO), about 76 million cases of foodborne disorders, 3,25,000 hospitalizations, and 5000 deaths occur every year [2]. Globally, scientists/researchers have continuously developed new antimicrobial molecules for the purpose of controlling Salmonella contaminations, but rapid detection of specific pathogens in food samples remains a challenge [5].

Numerous analytical methods have been developed to sense Salmonella; a detailed shortlist of Salmonella detection methods and their merits and limitations are tabulated in Table S1. According to the International Organization for Standardization (ISO), and International Food Health Agencies (IFHA), the classic culture technique (gold standard) is the standard for the detection of Salmonella [6,7]. However, this method involves laborious culture plating and an enrichment process on the selective medium and requires more than 4 days to obtain results [7]. A number of molecular sensing, immunological, and nucleic acid-based techniques have demonstrated specificity and sensitivity levels for Salmonella detection, superior to those of culture methods and take less time to perform [4,8,9]. However, these sensing techniques exhibit a variety of drawbacks such as the need to carry out sample pre-treatment, pre-enrichment steps, skilled personnel to handle the instrument, and high LODs (˂ 105 CFU/mL) with these shortfalls adversely affecting the target biomarkers [9]. After the above techniques were developed, activity-based fluorescence techniques were successfully applied for target detection and addressed most of the limitations outlined above [10]. Due to the turbidity of real whole blood and food samples, the fluorescence analyzer does not work in real solutions, and also requires sample pre-treatment [11,12]. In contrast, the activity-based electrochemical technique has delivered extraordinary results, working in turbid solutions and showing several advantages including ease of operation, high sensitivity, quick analysis, and portability [12–14].

In recent years, our research group has worked continuously on developing electrochemical latent ratiometric probes for the selective detection of non-redox active enzymes in clinically relevant biological cells [15–17]. The latent ratiometric switches consist of an active trigger group and a linker connected by a spacer. The trigger group is activated with a bio-analyte, causing the molecule to undergo self-immolation to reveal the masked electrochemical reporter followed by its translation into a spectral signal [12,15–17]. Recently, our group designed an activity-based latent ratiometric electrochemical probe, named “Sal-CAF”, for specifically achieving simple, rapid, and selective profiling of live Salmonella [12]. The Sal-CAF showed acceptable electrochemical results, including interference-free, linear wide-range concentrations of 1.03 × 105 to 1.1 × 1010 CFU/mL, LOD of 39.27 × 103 CFU/mL Sal. E and practical utility of turbid milk sample analysis. However, this method does not achieve low-level concentrations, detection limit and requires relatively long co-incubation times, and this feature is due to poor active sites on the unmodified glassy carbon electrode [12]. The International Food Safety Association (IFSA) has a great deal of difficulty in achieving rapid and easy "zero-tolerance or absence of microbes" detection of Salmonella pathogens in food materials [18–20]. Hence, there is an urgent need to develop simple and rapid analytical detection of live Salmonella for diagnosis and food surveillance.

In light of the above-described points, our previous work was further extended and propose that it has addressed all of the aforementioned shortfalls. As nanomaterials continue to develop, the detection time of Salmonella is expected to be greatly reduced while the sensitivity of the sensor can be significantly enhanced [21–23]. GQDs are a carbon family of zero-dimensional materials that have received considerable attention for use in biosensors because of their unique physicochemical properties [24]. Furthermore, different surface functionalities of GQDs provide excellent biocompatibility, electrical conductivity, aqueous solubility, and easy functionalization due to their abundant oxygen-rich chemical functional sites [24,25]. GQDs can interact with various biomolecules and other substrates via chemical net-charge interactions, and also play a powerful key role as an “auto-reducing agent” [24,25]. Among the various metal nanoparticles, AuNPs exhibit a potential key candidate for biosensors due to their tunable functional properties [26]. The GQDs have also been combined with AuNPs in order to boost their catalytic capabilities including the sensing of biomolecules, proteins, and live cells [4,26]. In addition to their biocatalytic properties, AuNPs are superior peroxidase-like electrocatalysts, due to their ability to enhance electron transfer and increase electrode surface area [26,27]. Moreover, the synergistic or combinational effect of AuNPs with GQDs could potentially enhance the activity of a biosensor in view of their significant electrochemical, and optical properties (Table S2) [27]. Moreover, AuNPs act as a key potential electrocatalyst for reversible redox reactions and an effective transducer for target biomolecules [28,29]. Thus, a hybrid nanocomposite electrode coupled with a latent probe could be used to detect targets with high sensitivity, selectivity, and help to maintain public health and safety.

In this study, a GQDs-AuNPs-modified electrode coupled with Sal-CAF was successfully developed and applied for the ultrasensitive detection of live Salmonella. Different analytical spectroscopic techniques were effectively used to analyze and confirm the nanocomposite material. The nanoelectrocatalyst served as a potential transducer and strong interfacial mediator between the electrode surface and pristine CAF from the unmasking latent target (Sal-CAF). The present hybrid system was able to measure in a linear manner, a wide range of concentrations, low detection limit, short analysis time, and “zero-tolerance” concentration achievement. As a result, the use of GQDs-AuNPs coupled with Sal-CAF has greatly improved the detection of live Salmonella and has overcome the shortfalls of our previous report described in Scheme 1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of the latent organic probe, graphene quantum dots and nanocomposite
ce:section.ce:para: A brief description of the chemicals, reagents, and instrumentations used to synthesize the Sal-CAF, GQDs, and their nanocomposite is included in the electronic supplementary information (ESI) (Fig. S1; SI. 1–4). The GQDs were prepared by thermal pyrolysis [24]. A clean glass bottle containing 2.5 g of L-glutamic acid (L-Glu) was gently heated at 210ᵒC (heating rate- 5ᵒC/min) with a conventional heating mantle. As the temperature increased at an elevated level, the solid phase of L-Glu slowly melted, followed by the colorless solution becoming brown within 30 s after the liquification. Next, the liquified brown-colored mixture was gradually cooled to atmospheric temperature followed by the addition of 20 mL of deionized water (DI-H2O). For the quality of GQDs, the mixture was ultracentrifuged for 25 min at 8000 RPM. The yield of GQDs were calculated and obtained in the weight range of 88.94 %. Finally, clear GQDs were collected in a glass vial and stored at ambient temperature. On the other hand, the latent organic probe (Sal-CAF) was synthesized by using the same route as our previous work [12], and a detailed synthetic procedure is provided in ESI (SI.3).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Assay method for the detection of live Salmonella
ce:section.ce:para: In order to detect and continuously monitor live Salmonella, about 1 mL of a mixture containing 50 µM of Sal-CAF (DMSO: PBS, 5 % v/v) in 0.2 M PBS (pH 7.4) was co-incubated with 1.0 × 103 CFU/mL to 1.0 × 1010 CFU/mL of live Salmonella containing 20 % of cell medium. These mixtures were co-incubated at 37ᵒC for 4 h, the mixture was transferred into an electrochemical working cell, and the current signal of CAF (signal-ON) was monitored by acquiring cyclic voltammetry (CV) and differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) (Fig. S1). A brief description of the assay sample preparation of various pathogenic bacteria, eleven different serotypes of Salmonella bacteria, human whole blood samples, live-dead cells derived from chemically and thermally treated routes, and dry emphasized Salmonella from glass substrate is included in ESI (SI. 5–8).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical and morphological elucidation of GQDs, and GQDs-AuNPs
ce:section.ce:para: A detailed description of the optimization and synthetic preparation of GQDs-AuNPs and their characteristics as measured by UV-Vis, photoluminescence, HR-TEM images of GQDs, AuNPs and results were included in ESI (Figs. S2, 3; SI. 9–11). Fig. 1 shows the HR-TEM images acquired of the GQDs-AuNPs at higher magnifications (a-c). The GQDs-AuNPs showed a well-ordered spherical shape with a uniform topology. Based on the histogram profile from HR-TEM images of GQDs-AuNPs, the average particle diameter was found to be 14.25 ± 0.2 nm and the size distribution of the particles was 2–7 nm. The diameter of the AuNPs is closely related to the absorption peak, and our results were found to be in good agreement with those in the published reports [30,31]. Ngo and co-workers have reported that the size of AuNPs in the range of 14–18 nm was observed in the wavelength range 518–542 nm [30]. Fig. 1d displays the selected area (electron) diffraction (SAED) pattern acquired from the GQDs-AuNPs. The GQDs-AuNPs yielded strong discrete diffraction spots with Miller indices of hkl (110), (200), (210), and (310), respectively [31,32]. The high clarity and well-ordered discrete nature of the spots showed that the nanocomposites were highly crystalline. The elemental composition results with a corresponding discussion of EDX are given in ESI (Fig. S4; SI.12).

FT-IR and XRD analyses of the GQDs-AuNPs were carried out, and details of the results are given in ESI (Figs. S5, 6; SI. 13, 14) (Table S3). The XPS analysis was also carried out to provide a detailed information of the electronic state of the GQDs-AuNPs and to confirm their metallic state. This analysis of GQDs-AuNPs showed the presence of carbon, oxygen, nitrogen, and gold. Fig. 2a displays the deconvoluted XPS result of the Au 4f regions obtained at 81.7 and 85.3 eV and these peaks were assigned to Au 4f7/2 and Au 4f5/2, respectively [32]. The difference between the spin-orbit splitting of two energy levels is 3.60 eV which was consistent with the successfully confirmed that metallic in nature [32]. The C 1s region of the spectrum showed peaks at three different energy levels, namely at 283.7, 284.3, and 287.0 eV, confirming the presence of -C-C/-CC, -C-N, and -CO bonds, respectively (Fig. 2b) [32]. The N 1s region of the spectrum showed deconvoluted peaks at 397.3, 398.6, and 399.4 eV, which were assigned to -NH, -NH2, and -C-N- (Fig. 2c). Finally, the O 1s region of the spectrum exhibited at 528.7 and 530.1 eV, values confirming the presence of -CO, and -C-O-C- (Fig. 2d).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical behavior of Sal-CAF coupled with GQDs-AuNPs modified electrode to sense Salmonella

ce:section.ce:para: Detailed descriptions of the electrode fabrication (Fig. S7; SI.15) and their advantages of GQDs-AuNPs/GCE are listed in Table S4. The spectral graph of CVs, interfacial charge transfer behaviors of the control GCE and modified electrodes, and their corresponding results were included in ESI (Figs. S8,9) (SI.16). The voltammetric current signal response for the electroactive latent probe of Sal-CAF under the modified electrocatalyst electrode. Fig. S10A shows the CVs for 50 µM of Sal-CAF (DMSO/PBS, 5 % v/v) on a bare GCE and GQDs-AuNPs/GCE at a scan rate of 50 mV/s. The bare GCE yielded a broad oxidation peak at + 0.29 V (vs. Ag/AgCl) as well as a reduction peak at + 0.26 V, attributed to the redox behavior of the electroactive functional site of ferrocene carbamate (unmasking Sal-CAF; signal-off) (Fig. S10A, curve a) [12,33]. While a similar and sharp current behavior was observed when using GQDs-AuNPs/GCE, specifically peaks at + 0.31 V, and + 0.25 V (Fig. S10A, curve b). Compared with the bare GCE, the modified electrode showed > 11-fold and 6-fold higher redox currents, which enabled facile electron transfer kinetics, and had a three-fold higher active surface area; hence it served as a good transducer [34]. The DPV analysis also yielded similar current responses to CVs (Fig. S10B). The voltammetry results showed a broad peak at + 0.29 V (GCE) (curve a) with less pronounced current, and a sharp peak at + 0.24 V (GQDs-AuNPs/GCE) (curve b) with a less positive peak potential and enhanced current signal.

Next, in order to evaluate the selective electrocatalytic behavior of live Salmonella, namely GQDs-AuNPs/GCE coupled with the latent probe Sal-CAF. The catalytic response was demonstrated by acquiring CVs ( Fig. 3 A) and DPVs (Fig. 3B) of GQDs-AuNPs/GCE in the presence of redox-active Sal-CAF with co-incubation of Salmonella enteritidis (Sal. E) for 4 h (20 % broth medium); detailed voltammetric results are included in ESI (Fig. S11). In the presence of 1010 CFU/mL Sal. E yielded a newly arisen redox peak at − 0.15 V (Figs. 3 A, B curve c), the characteristic peak of pristine CAF ( Fig. 4 A, B, curve b), and a decrease in the current signal of the positive peak at + 0.24 V (Figs. 3 A, B, curve a). The bare GCE showed a similar behavior but with poor current responses (Figs. 3 A, B, curve d), specifically with 4.5-fold less intense current responses than the GQDs-AuNPs/GCE (Fig. 3B). The GQDs-AuNPs modified electrode coupled with a latent probe successfully enhanced the current signal response of Salmonella via unmasking Sal-CAF (signal-off) conversion into CAF (signal-on) (Fig. S12).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ultrasensitive detection performance of live Salmonella
ce:section.ce:para: DPV is a sensitive analytical technique and it is more suitable for achieving quantitative monitoring of Salmonella under an optimal working condition [17]. In the absence of Sal E, a sharp peak occurred at + 0.24 V (Sal-CAF) when using the GQDs-AuNPs-modified electrode, with this voltammetric peak attributed to active functional sites of ferrocene carbamate (red line) (Fig. 3 C). Interestingly, when Sal. E (103 CFU/mL) was introduced in the working system, a new lower peak potential and less pronounced current response were detected at − 0.15 V (signal of CAF); in addition, the Sal-CAF (+ 0.24 V) current signal was decreased (curve a). As the concentration of Sal. E was increased from 103 CFU/mL to 1010 CFU/mL, the current signal of the peak at − 0.15 V increased, while that of the Sal-CAF signal at + 0.24 V was decreased to relatively zero (curves b to s). The present sensor signal was not affected by potential peak shifts or signal noise with increases in Sal. E concentration. The plot of current response of CAF versus the concentration of Sal. E was observed to be linear, with a regression coefficient value R2 of 0.9913 (Fig. 3D). The calculated LOD was found to be 35.62 × 101 CFU/mL based on the standard method (3σ/slope). To the best of our knowledge, the present sensor showed a wider range of measurable concentrations, lower detection limit, and shorter co-incubation time than the previously reported methods based on chemiluminescence (LOD = 28.8 × 103 CFU/mL, 6 h), fluorescence (LOD = 2.2 × 103 CFU/mL, 4–6 h) or electrochemistry (LOD = 39.27 × 103 CFU/mL, 7 h) (Table S1). In comparison to the system described in our previous report, the present system showed an order-of-magnitude (101) lower detection limit with a shorter co-incubation time. There are several factors that may have led to the higher electrocatalytic activity of Sal. E using the GQDs-AuNPs modified electrode with a Sal-CAF, and their possible explanations, along with schematic representations are provided in ESI (Fig. S13; SI. 17).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of selectivity, serotype Salmonella bacteria and virulent live from non-virulent dead Salmonella analysis
ce:section.ce:para: The selectivity of electrochemical biosensors is an important parameter for qualitative analysis. A combination of GQDs-AuNPs/GCE and Sal-CAF was tested to determine their selectivity for live Salmonella in the presence of potentially interfering various pathogens. The electrode responds (turn-on; signal of CAF) exclusively to live Salmonella but not to any other pathogenic bacteria (Fig. 4 A). Initially, the electroactive probe of Sal-CAF was successfully co-incubated with negative and esterase-based positive controls of pathogens in a suitable cell medium for 4 h (Fig. S14). The developed sensing device in the presence of Sal. T and Sal. E showed a current response at negative peak potential (− 0.15 V for CAF) (Fig. 4 A (bar chart)). Esterase-positive controls of E. coli and S. aureus as well as negative controls of B. subtilis and L. acidophilus yielded a near background current signal, due to their lack of the apeE gene (Fig. S14). The C8-esterase is expressed on the outer membrane of Sal. T and Sal. E, but is not present in other pathogenic bacteria [12]. Based on the obtained DPV results, it was clear that the current system was able to selectively detect Salmonella-based pathogens in chemical and clinical laboratories. Next, a series of eleven Salmonella pathogens were then tested using GQDs-AuNPs/GCE and the Sal. CAF. All eleven serotypes of Salmonella pathogens were able to be unmasked by Sal-CAF and a detailed description of the voltammetric spectral data and their corresponding results is included in ESI (Fig. S15; SI. 18).

From a clinical perspective, the distinction between live and dead Salmonella is crucial in food and pathogen-contaminated areas due to preventing infections [35]. Therefore, selective monitoring of live and dead Salmonella is highly essential. The selectivity of GQDs-AuNPs/GCE coupled with Sal-CAF was tested by monitoring its sensing performance for Salmonella cells. In the control experiment, 50 µM of Sal-CAF was co-incubated with 106 CFU/mL of Sal. E under an optimized working condition at 37ᵒC (Fig. 4B). The CAF current signal at − 0.15 V increased with increasing contact time. Salmonella cells were deactivated in two ways: Chemical sanitizing treatments using NaOCl, polymyxin B sulfate, and thermal treatment. The Sal-CAF with Salmonella (106 CFU/mL) treated with 50 ppm of NaOCl (green curve), heat (90ᵒC for 30 min, curve red), and polymyxin B sulfate (1000 units/mL, brown curve) elicited no notable current signal responses. Based on these results, the present system is clearly capable of distinguishing both live and dead Salmonella cells.

According to the National Center for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC), HAIs constitute one of the top 10 causes of death in the United States. Up to 10 % of hospital patients may acquire a HAI, which leads to an additional cost of $5.5 billion per year for patient clinical treatment [36]. These infections are especially important because they frequently result from healthcare-associated infections [36]. In the past few years, alternative culture-independent molecular-based assessments, namely polymerase chain reaction (PCR) and q-PCR detection techniques, have been widely used to identify the dead cells of various food and environmental pathogen bacteria with the help of external DNA-intercalating reagents [37]. These techniques are prone to overestimation of bacterial viability and often lead to false-positive results, due to the molecular sensing targets such as DNA or antigen of the dead bacteria present in an extracellular liquid being indistinguishable from those of living cells [37]. In general, molecular-based cultural methods do not provide accurate results for detecting pathogen contamination at an earlier stage. In addition, the PCR detection technique is more sensitive, but it is highly dependent on temperature, storage, analytical reagents, and a long range of detection time. Due to these interstice parameters, this system was unsuitable for the analysis of pathogens. Alternatively, the GQDs-AuNPs/GCE with Sal-CAF allows continuous monitoring of Salmonella with a simple co-incubation procedure.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Dynamic monitoring and zero-tolerance detection of live Salmonella
ce:section.ce:para: The majority of pathogen detection techniques have been developed, but they are not capable of detecting trace amounts of contamination or preventing exceedances. The present dynamic monitoring method was expected to have the potential to monitor samples after the onset of Salmonella contamination when the bacterial levels are still low. In order to evaluate this expectation, various cell counts of Salmonella were co-incubated with Sal-CAF and monitored the electrochemical signal responses using GQDs-AuNPs/GCE ( Fig. 5). Initially, Sal-CAF (50 µM) co-incubated with broth medium alone at 37ᵒC for 1–16 h, as the control experiment exhibited an absence (background current 10−9 A) of current responses at − 0.15 V (signal-off), and a plot of results of the dynamic monitoring of current responses at − 0.15 V versus time is shown in Fig. 5 A, curve a. Compared to the control results, in the presence of Sal E. (100 CFU/mL), an interesting result was observed at − 0.15 V (CAF) after the 8 h interval, and an exciting result due to a limited activation of the latent switch (Sal-CAF) using the modified electrode (GQDs-AuNPs/GCE) (curve a) (Fig. S16). As the contact time was increased to 16 h, the current signal gradually improved and strengthened (Fig. S16). A plot of the dynamic monitoring of Sal E. (100 CFU/mL), the current signal response of CAF versus contact time, is shown in Fig. 5 A, curve b. It was interesting to observe the plot results since no reports were found. The obtained current signal was indirectly reporting the limited number of participations of Salmonella colonies. Further, the present system was extended to Sal. E concentrations from 101 CFU/mL to 106 CFU/mL for 0–16 h (Fig. 5, curves c to g) (Figs. S17-19), a strong signal was observed within an hour at concentrations above 103 CFU/mL (Fig. 5 A).

In order to validate the electrochemical results, the standard plating method was used. Before moving on to the Sal. E, a control experiment was conducted and no colonies were observed (Fig. 5B, a). Afterward, 100 and 101 CFU/mL of Sal. E was applied to the plating method, and colonies were detected after 72 h. The calculated colonies are 4 and 39 in the presence of 100 and 101 CFU/0.5 mL of Sal. E (Fig. 5B, b, and c). The plot result shows time vs. concentration of Sal. E (Fig. 5 C). It is interesting to note that the present system displayed a signal response and rapidly reached a threshold current of 0.01 µA (10 X the background current (∼ 109 A). As the concentration increased, signaling times decreased significantly, easily reaching the threshold point. As a result of using GQDs-AuNPs/GCE coupled with Sal-CAF, we were able to detect 100 CFU/mL at 12 h, and 105 CFU/mL within 1 h compared to our previous work (103 CFU/mL; 12 h; GCE) [12]. The co-incubation time of the modified electrode system was significantly decreased six-fold compared to the unmodified surface (GCE). The electrochemical system was highly applicable to the continuous monitoring of live Salmonella and easily reached the threshold point when using GQDs-AuNPs/GCE with Sal-CAF. It was interesting to note that the modified electrode with Sal-CAF was able to detect 100 and 101 CFU/mL of Sal. E within the time periods of 12 h and 10 h, as opposed to 72 h for the standard plating method. Finally, the present electrochemical method produced superior results in a timely manner compared to standard plating technique.

From a biological standpoint, identifying the key factors in trace level determination of live pathogenic bacteria is challenging [38]. Globally, foodborne illnesses constitute a major health problem. Salmonella enterica is a Gram-negative bacterium commonly found in poultry, resulting in nearly a quarter of all foodborne illness cases each year [39]. The conventional methods for detecting Salmonella lack the sensitivity, hence necessary to detect the small number of pathogenic cells that can cause illness. The incubation time was extended to 48 h, and detecting the pathogen within the time limit is crucial for treating patients with foodborne illnesses and possibly preventing further spread and consumption of contaminated food [39]. In order to safely decrease product holding time, food processors must reduce test time without altering sensitivity. The present dynamic monitoring approach was simple to operate and able to offer information at the initial level of contamination, which can be used to determine acceptable levels of environmental bacteria in a high-risk environment. The present electrochemical platform was successfully employed to directly detect and quantify live Salmonella with minimal pretreatment effort, ease of performance, time-saving, and high sensitivity. Finally, the simple and rapid detection of the present approach is applicable for POC detection to achieve the "zero-tolerance” target for sensing Salmonella in food surveillance applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Real-time detection of Salmonella in a simulated solid surface and human whole blood analysis
ce:section.ce:para: The present work was extended to monitor dry emphasized Salmonella recovered from the surfaces of food or environmental samples according to Association of Analytical Communities (AOAC) guidelines [40]. Two different concentrations of Salmonella were applied to a glass surface and then dried for 3 h. Followed by collecting the dried Salmonella samples by swabbing them with cotton balls. In contrast to the procedure used in our previously reported work, the swabbed samples were immersed directly (without a pre-enrichment process) into cell broth followed by being co-incubated with Sal-CAF under optimized conditions ( Fig. 6 A). Then, the co-incubated solution was transferred to electrochemical cells, and showed a signal at − 0.15 V for activation of CAF. In the control experiment, no measurable current response was observed (Fig. 6B) (Fig. S20). The concentrations of 102 CFU/mL and 104 CFU/mL of Sal. E exhibited significant current responses at a peak potential of − 0.15 V (signal-CAF) from the unmasked Sal-CAF (Fig. S20). The obtained these interesting results only appear in the present system, because our previous work required a pre-enrichment treatment for 16 h, following a swabbing of dry samples. The use of the dry concentration method in the present system allowed us to achieve analysis of the target without any interactions of Sal-CAF with Salmonella, saving a lot of time. In summary, the modified electrocatalyst coupled with a latent probe can successfully monitor the target biomarker from dry emphasized Salmonella cells following universal guidelines, and it can be used in the real-time analysis of food samples.

Non-typhoidal Salmonella (NTS) serotypes cause blood-stream infection (BSI) and are an emerging yet neglected tropical disease [41]. It causes very high morbidity and mortality, as well as damage to the immune system, and makes them vulnerable infected persons to NTS [41,42]. Therefore, real-time practical applications of blood samples are critical, and detecting them in turbid environments presents a challenge. To monitor the signal response of live Salmonella in the absence of pre-treatment blood samples using GQDs-AuNPs/GCE coupled with Sal-CAF. A concentration of 20 % blood sample was found to be optimal for minimizing the matrix effect of the real sample and without any further pre-treatments. The whole blood sample was spiked into Sal. E (101 and 102 CFU/mL) at 37ᵒC, incubated with Sal-CAF, and the current responses of CAF were monitored using GQDs-AuNPs/GCE (Fig. 6 C) (Figs. S21A and B). In the absence of Sal. E, no current response was detected (background current response ∼ 1 × 109 A) (curve a). In the presence of Sal. E (101 CFU/mL and 102 CFU/mL), the current signal response of CAF at a peak potential of − 0.15 V was gradually strengthened as the incubation time was increased (curves b and c), and the detailed spectral data with corresponding discussions are included in Fig. S22. Additionally, the present system was extended to milk samples, with the results shown in Fig. S23. According to the above results, it was concluded that the present system was able to directly detect low levels of Salmonella in a turbid medium, and food samples. The present sensing system with its dynamic monitoring approach can be a promising simple tool for stable and highly reproducible detection of live Salmonella (Fig. S24).

Live Salmonella has been successfully detected using a GQDs-AuNPs modified electrode coupled with the latent redox probe Sal-CAF. In comparison with previous studies, the present system delivered excellent catalytic activity, including a lower detection peak potential, a separation between the peak positions of Sal-CAF and CAF, enhanced redox current, a linear wide range of concentrations, and shorter co-incubation time. Additionally, the modified electrode surface could detect targeted live Salmonella and also monitor different serotypes of Salmonella. A practical application of the present method was demonstrated in human whole blood and milk samples and also used to determine the concentration of Salmonella in dry emphasized samples. The present system was shown to be able to monitor and detect low levels of CFU (100 CFU) as well as achieve a "zero-tolerance" target for Salmonella.

Namasivayam Kumaragurubaran: Data curation, Formal analysis, Reviewing and editing. P. Arul: Data curation, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Sheng-Tung Huang: Supervision, Funding acquisition, Reviewing and editing.Chih-Hung Huang: Formal analysis, Bio-sample resources. Shiuh-Bin Fang: Formal analysis, Bio-sample resources. Ying-Hsiu Lin: Formal analysis.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are grateful for financial support from the Ministry of Science and Technology, Taiwan (MOST-107-2113-M-027-006 and MOST-108-2113-M-027-001). P. Arul would like to thank the National Taipei University of Technology for the position of Research Assistant Professor.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Additional experimental and characterization details were provided in supplementary information (PDF), available with free of charge. Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.snb.2023.133428.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material
.

Lunguya, Non-typhoidal Salmonella bloodstream infections in Kisantu, DR Congo: Emergence of O5-negative Salmonella Typhimurium and extensive drug resistance, PLOS Negl. Trop. Dis. 13 (2019) e0007293.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Namasivayam Kumaragurubaran studied B.S and M.S. Chemistry at Bishop Heber College in Trichy, Tamil Nadu, India. He currently doing his research under the supervision of Prof. Sheng-Tung Huang at the Department of Chemical Engineering and Biotechnology, Institute of Biochemical and Biomedical Engineering, National Taipei University of Technology, Taiwan-106. His research focuses on developing latent ratiometric organic probes and their electrochemical biosensing applications.

P. Arul is currently working as a Research Assistant Professor under the mentor of Prof. Sheng-Tung Huang at the National Taipei University of Technology, Taipei, Taiwan-106. His research focuses on the synthesis of various metallic and non-metallic organic frameworks and their hybrid nanocomposites for electrochemical biosensors, chemical sensors, enzymatic/non-enzymatic sensors, and real-time live cells practical applications. He published more than 25 peer-reviewed scientific publications in a well-reputed journal.

Sheng-Tung Huang is a Distinguished Professor at the Department of Chemical Engineering and Biotechnology, Institute of Biochemical and Biomedical Engineering, National Taipei University of Technology, Taipei, Taiwan-106. His research interests include developing innovative fluorogenic and electrochemical active redox molecules for biosensors, bioelectronics, and bio-imaging, synthesizing novel anti-cancer agents, designing and synthesizing DNA intercalators for electrochemical gene detection, and preparing new efficient water vapor impermeable packaging materials for a new generation of bendable solar cells and bio-electronic devices. He published over 110 peer-reviewed scientific publications in well-reputed SCI journals and presented numerous papers at national and international level conferences.

Chih-Hung Huang is an Assistant Professor at the Department of Chemical Engineering and Biotechnology, National Taipei University of Technology, Taipei, Taiwan-106. His research interests include bacterial genetics, molecular biology, and genetic engineering.

Shiuh-Bin Fang is the Associate Professor of Pediatrics and the incumbent Associate Chair in International Affairs, School of Medicine at Taipei Medical University. He is also the senior attending physician in pediatrics at Taipei Medical University, Shuang Ho Hospital. Dr. Fang’s studies focus on the four domains: (1) early detection of Salmonella and its antibiotic resistance, (2) effects of probiotics and health foods (e.g. cinnamon extracts) on human intestinal infections and decolonization of antibiotic-resistant gut bacteria, (3) pathogen-host interactions with human intestinal epithelium and novel Salmonella virulence genes using human intestinal in vitro organ cultures and in vitro M cell models, and (4) gut microbiota. The research directions evolve from Salmonella virulence genes towards antibiotic resistance genes, optimized probiotics for the decolonization of vancomycin-resistant enterococci (VRE), and microbiota-associated diseases, showing his great interest in translational medicine bridging basic and clinical sciences.

Ying-Hsiu Lin is currently working as a Researcher at Department of Pediatrics, Taipei Medical University, Taipei, Taiwan. Research interests include mRNA, sequencing, cloning, and molecular genetics.
